agent_output: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"The engineering team proposes reducing delivery ETA granularity from exact timestamps (e.g., 'Delivery by 14:30 on Friday') to date-only windows (e.g., 'Delivery by Friday'). This is a fundamental change to how the system communicates delivery expectations to end customers and downstream systems. ETA precision is one of the most sensitive customer-facing data points in logistics — it directly drives customer behavior (e.g., staying home for a delivery), operational planning, and contractual SLA measurement.","bounded_context":"Delivery Tracking / ETA Calculation","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"ETA","definition":"Estimated Time of Arrival — the system's prediction of when a parcel will be delivered to the recipient."},{"term":"Delivery Window","definition":"The time range within which the recipient can expect delivery. Can be expressed as a precise timestamp, a time range (e.g., 9am-12pm), or a date-only target."},{"term":"SLA","definition":"Service Level Agreement — contractual commitment on delivery timeliness, often measured against the ETA communicated to the customer."},{"term":"Last Mile","definition":"The final leg of delivery from the local depot/hub to the recipient's address. The segment where ETA precision matters most."},{"term":"Cutoff Time","definition":"The latest time within an ETA date that still counts as on-time delivery. Critical when moving from timestamp to date-only — does 'by Friday' mean by 11:59 PM Friday or by end of business Friday?"}]},"business_rules":[{"id":"BR-1","rule":"Every shipment in transit must have a customer-visible ETA that is updated as conditions change.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"A shipment without an ETA is a broken tracking experience. The system must always project a delivery expectation — the format may change but the obligation to provide one cannot.","testable_assertion":"ASSERT that every shipment with status IN_TRANSIT has a non-null ETA value visible to the customer."},{"id":"BR-2","rule":"ETA precision must be sufficient to measure SLA compliance. If the SLA promises delivery 'by 14:30', a date-only ETA cannot determine whether the SLA was met or breached.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"SLA compliance is a contractual obligation. If the system cannot measure it, the business cannot enforce or report on it.","testable_assertion":"ASSERT that the ETA granularity is at least as precise as the SLA measurement granularity for every service tier."},{"id":"BR-3","rule":"Changing ETA format on in-flight shipments must not retroactively alter SLA measurement for those shipments.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Changing the measurement criteria mid-delivery would corrupt compliance data and could constitute a contractual violation.","testable_assertion":"ASSERT that shipments created before the format change retain their original ETA precision for SLA calculation until delivered."},{"id":"BR-4","rule":"Internal operational systems (dispatch, routing, driver apps) may retain timestamp-level precision even if the customer-facing display is date-only.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"This is an implementation strategy, not a hard constraint. Operations may choose to expose more or less precision internally.","testable_assertion":"ASSERT that the internal ETA calculation engine continues to produce timestamp-precision ETAs regardless of customer-facing display format."},{"id":"BR-5","rule":"Premium or time-definite service tiers (e.g., same-day, next-day by 10:30 AM) must not lose ETA precision if their value proposition depends on it.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"A premium service tier that promises delivery by a specific time but only shows a date has broken its value proposition. Customers pay more for time-definite guarantees.","testable_assertion":"ASSERT that service tiers with time-definite SLAs continue to display timestamp-level ETAs to customers."}],"acceptance_criteria":[{"id":"AC-1","given":"A shipment on a standard (non-time-definite) service tier is in transit","when":"The customer views the tracking page","then":"The ETA is displayed as a date-only value (e.g., 'Delivery by Friday, March 27') with no specific time shown."},{"id":"AC-2","given":"A shipment on a time-definite premium service tier (e.g., next-day-by-10:30) is in transit","when":"The customer views the tracking page","then":"The ETA retains timestamp precision (e.g., 'Delivery by 10:30 AM on Friday') consistent with the service promise."},{"id":"AC-3","given":"A shipment was created before the ETA format migration with a timestamp-precision ETA","when":"The migration goes live while the shipment is still in transit","then":"The shipment's SLA compliance is still calculated against the original timestamp-precision ETA, not the new date-only format."},{"id":"AC-4","given":"The internal dispatch/routing system calculates ETAs","when":"The customer-facing format changes to date-only","then":"Internal systems continue to operate on timestamp-precision ETAs for route optimization and driver scheduling."},{"id":"AC-5","given":"A shipment's ETA date is today","when":"The customer views tracking in the morning vs. the evening","then":"The display clearly communicates the delivery is expected today without implying a specific time window, and updates to 'Delivery attempted' or next-day if the window passes."}],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"Shipment crosses midnight in the recipient's timezone during delivery","expected_behavior":"ETA date must be calculated in the recipient's local timezone, not the origin or system timezone. A package arriving at 00:15 Saturday must not show 'Delivery by Friday' to the customer."},{"scenario":"Customer has an API integration that parses the ETA timestamp for warehouse receiving automation","expected_behavior":"The API contract must not break. If the API previously returned an ISO-8601 timestamp, switching to date-only is a breaking change. API consumers need a migration path or the API must continue returning timestamp precision."},{"scenario":"Multiple delivery attempts — first attempt fails on the ETA date","expected_behavior":"The ETA must update to the next delivery attempt date, not continue showing the original date that has already passed."},{"scenario":"'Delivery by Friday' when Friday is a holiday or non-operating day in the recipient's region","expected_behavior":"The system must account for operating calendars. If Friday is non-operational, the ETA should show the next valid delivery date."},{"scenario":"B2B customer with receiving dock hours (e.g., 8am-4pm only)","expected_behavior":"Date-only ETA may be insufficient for B2B recipients who need to staff receiving docks. Business recipients may require time-window precision regardless of service tier."}],"business_impact":{"financial":"Reduced ETA precision could lower customer-perceived value, particularly for premium tiers. If premium customers lose time-definite visibility, churn risk increases. Conversely, if current timestamp ETAs are frequently inaccurate (causing complaints/refunds), moving to date-only could reduce refund claims by widening the 'on-time' window. SLA penalty exposure changes significantly — a date-only SLA is easier to meet than a timestamp SLA, potentially reducing penalty payouts to enterprise clients but also reducing competitive differentiation.","operational":"Operationally, date-only ETAs reduce pressure on last-mile optimization — drivers have a full-day window rather than a specific time slot. This could simplify routing but may also reduce urgency and degrade actual delivery speed over time. Dispatch and routing systems should retain internal timestamp precision regardless. The migration itself carries operational risk for in-flight shipments.","user":"This is the highest-impact dimension. Customers have built expectations around timestamp ETAs. Date-only ETAs are objectively less useful — recipients cannot plan their day around 'by Friday.' This is especially painful for residential deliveries where someone must be home. Customer satisfaction (CSAT) and NPS are likely to decrease unless the current timestamps are already unreliable. If timestamps were frequently wrong, a vaguer-but-accurate date may actually improve trust.","risk":"Breaking change risk for API consumers who parse timestamps. SLA measurement methodology change could trigger contract renegotiations with enterprise clients. Regulatory risk if operating in jurisdictions with consumer delivery transparency requirements. Reputational risk if perceived as a service downgrade. Data migration risk for in-flight shipments during cutover.","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"End customers (B2C residential)","impact":"Lose ability to plan around a specific delivery time. Must be available all day or risk missed delivery. High negative impact unless current timestamps were unreliable."},{"group":"End customers (B2B with receiving docks)","impact":"Potentially severe — dock scheduling requires time precision. Date-only may cause operational disruption at receiving facilities."},{"group":"Enterprise/contract clients","impact":"SLA measurement methodology changes. May trigger contract renegotiations. Clients parsing ETAs via API face a breaking change."},{"group":"Operations/Dispatch teams","impact":"Internal precision should be retained, so direct impact is low. However, reduced customer-facing precision may shift complaint patterns."},{"group":"Customer support teams","impact":"Inquiry volume may spike initially ('when exactly will it arrive?'). Support scripts and SLAs need updating."},{"group":"Product/Competitive positioning","impact":"Moving to less precise ETAs is counter to the industry trend toward more precision (e.g., Amazon's delivery windows). Could be perceived as a step backward."},{"group":"Finance/Revenue teams","impact":"SLA penalty calculations change. Refund policy for late deliveries needs redefinition — what constitutes 'late' with a date-only ETA?"}]},"confidence":{"level":45,"reasoning":"The proposal is stated as a blanket change without providing the business motivation, service tier differentiation strategy, current ETA accuracy metrics, API contract details, SLA contract language, or customer segment breakdown. Multiple critical pieces of context were never provided by anyone in the scenario, making it impossible to confidently assess whether this is a reasonable simplification of unreliable timestamps or a damaging loss of precision.","high_confidence_areas":["ETA is a core domain concept in logistics — its precision directly impacts customer experience and SLA measurement","Time-definite service tiers must not lose precision","In-flight shipments need migration protection","API contract is a breaking change concern","B2B recipients have different precision needs than B2C"],"low_confidence_areas":["Current ETA accuracy rates — if timestamps are already unreliable, date-only may be an improvement","Service tier definitions and which tiers are time-definite vs standard","API contract structure and whether external integrations parse timestamp fields","Enterprise SLA contract language — whether it references timestamps or dates","The business motivation driving this change — cost reduction, accuracy improvement, or something else"],"assumptions":["The system has multiple service tiers, some of which are time-definite","Internal systems will retain timestamp precision even if customer-facing display changes","There are existing API consumers who may parse ETA timestamps","The change applies to customer-facing display, not internal calculations"]},"escalations":[{"type":"missing_context","description":"The business motivation for this change was never stated. Is this driven by inaccurate timestamps causing customer complaints? Cost reduction in ETA calculation infrastructure? Simplification of SLA measurement? The correct domain analysis depends heavily on WHY this change is proposed. A simplification of unreliable data is very different from a deliberate precision reduction.","affected_stakeholders":["Product team","Engineering team","All downstream stakeholders"],"options":["Provide current ETA accuracy metrics (% of timestamp ETAs that were accurate within 30 min)","Clarify the business problem this change solves","Share customer complaint data related to ETA accuracy"],"recommendation":"Before approving this change, the team must articulate the business problem. If current timestamps are >80% accurate, reducing precision is likely a net negative. If <50% accurate, date-only may genuinely improve customer trust."},{"type":"missing_context","description":"Service tier definitions and their SLA commitments were never provided. A blanket change to date-only ETAs cannot be evaluated without knowing which tiers promise time-definite delivery. The acceptability of this change is completely different for a 'standard ground' tier vs a 'next-day by 10:30 AM' tier.","affected_stakeholders":["Enterprise clients","Premium service customers","Finance/SLA teams"],"options":["Provide the full list of service tiers and their SLA definitions","Clarify whether the change applies to all tiers or only standard/economy tiers","Share the SLA contract language for enterprise clients"],"recommendation":"At minimum, time-definite service tiers must be excluded from this change. The team should provide the tier matrix before any implementation begins."},{"type":"missing_context","description":"No information was provided about existing API contracts or external integrations that consume ETA data. Changing from timestamp to date-only in an API response is a breaking change that could disrupt downstream systems (e.g., warehouse management systems, customer notification integrations).","affected_stakeholders":["Enterprise API consumers","Integration partners","B2B customers with automated receiving"],"options":["Audit current API consumers and their ETA field usage","Provide API contract/schema documentation","Plan a versioned API migration path"],"recommendation":"Conduct an API consumer impact assessment before implementation. If any consumers parse the timestamp field, a versioned migration with deprecation notice is required."}],"rejection_reasons":[{"violation":"Blanket removal of timestamp precision across all service tiers without service-tier differentiation","business_rule_broken":"BR-5: Premium or time-definite service tiers must not lose ETA precision if their value proposition depends on it."},{"violation":"No migration strategy articulated for in-flight shipments","business_rule_broken":"BR-3: Changing ETA format on in-flight shipments must not retroactively alter SLA measurement."}]}

This is a fundamental change to how the system communicates delivery expectations to end customers and downstream systems. ETA precision is one of the most sensitive customer-facing data points in logistics — it directly drives customer behavior (e.g., staying home for a delivery), op

content.thinking:
Let me analyze this ETA change proposal from a domain authority perspective.

This is a logistics domain question about changing ETA precision from exact timestamps to date-only windows. Let me think through this carefully.

Key observations:
- No codebase to inspect, this is a pure domain analysis
- The proposal lacks critical details: WHY the change? What problem does it solve? What are the SLAs?
- There's ambiguity: "date-only windows" vs "Delivery by Friday" - is this a window or a deadline?

Let me classify: The scenario describes a change but doesn't provide the business rationale, current SLA commitments, customer segment information, or what drove this decision. 

I'm missing too much context to give a proper assessment—I need to understand what problem this change is actually trying to solve, what the existing commitments are, and whether this impacts customer-facing promises.
